content:
AUDIENCE:
The reader is an 8-year-old child who is curious about how their body works. They likely know basic science ideas like cells being tiny parts of the body and that the body needs food and water to live. They might have heard about DNA as something inside cells but don't know much about it or about mRNA. They care about fun, simple explanations that relate to things they can imagine, like instructions or messages. Complex scientific words or too many details about molecules or chemistry would confuse or bore them. A friendly, playful, and clear tone that uses comparisons to everyday things will work best.

===BEGIN===
Inside your body, you have tiny parts called cells, and each cell is like a busy factory. Inside this factory, there is a special set of instructions called DNA, which tells the cell how to make everything it needs to work. But the DNA can’t leave the special room where it lives, so it sends a helper called mRNA to carry the instructions to other parts of the cell.

Think of mRNA like a messenger with a recipe. It takes the recipe from the DNA and travels to the kitchen of the cell, where the cell makes proteins. These proteins help your body grow, fix itself, and stay healthy. So, mRNA is super important because it helps the cell know exactly what to make, just like a recipe helps a cook know what to bake!

Isn’t it cool how your body has tiny messengers working all the time to keep you strong?
===END===
